title: Search Innovation India
subtitle: Reimagining information discovery for the diverse Indian web user.
problem_statement: The current landscape, exemplified by the visually minimalist Google.com homepage with its central text search bar, lacks a unified, context-aware, and truly multilingual information discovery platform tailored for India's diverse user base, leading to fragmented access and suboptimal search experiences for many.

pillars.title: Accessibility & Inclusivity
pillars.insight: Users in India often face language barriers and varying digital literacy levels, requiring intuitive interfaces and robust multilingual support beyond simple language selection links.

pillars.title: Relevance & Context
pillars.insight: Information needs are highly contextual; generic search results from a global engine may not resonate with local cultural nuances or specific regional queries without explicit user effort.

pillars.title: Efficiency & Speed
pillars.insight: Users expect instant, accurate results, especially on mobile-first platforms with potentially inconsistent internet connectivity, demanding streamlined input and output.

artifacts.label: Strategy
artifacts.title: Defining the Challenges
questions: How might we create a dominant, localized search engine that addresses India's unique linguistic and digital literacy challenges, moving beyond a text-first paradigm?
How might we empower Indian users to find relevant information quickly and accurately, regardless of language or input method, by leveraging multimodal interactions?
How might we leverage emerging technologies and local cultural nuances to redefine the search experience for the next billion Indian internet users, making it intuitively accessible?

data.name: Google Search (Current UI)
pros: Dominant market share, familiar UI (Google logo, central search bar, 'Google Search' button)
Offers multilingual options (e.g., 'Google offered in: Hindi Bengali Telugu...')
Integrated services (Gmail, Images, Google apps icon)
cons: Primarily text-based input, potentially alienating non-English speakers or low-literacy users
Generic global results may lack local Indian context without specific query refinement
UI, while clean, doesn't explicitly highlight advanced local features for India

data.name: Local Directories/Forums (e.g., Justdial)
pros: Highly localized and specific information for services and businesses
cons: Poor search functionality, often requiring manual browsing
Limited scope and reach compared to a comprehensive search engine

data.name: Social Media Platforms (e.g., WhatsApp, YouTube)
pros: High user engagement and reach in India, especially for voice interaction
Rich media content (videos, images) for information discovery
Voice-based interaction common (e.g., WhatsApp voice notes)
cons: Information is often unstructured and difficult to search systematically
Prone to misinformation and echo chambers
Not designed for comprehensive, reliable information retrieval

points.name: Google Search (Current)
points.desc: Global search, some localization, but still text-heavy for primary input.

points.name: Local Directories
points.desc: Highly localized but often clunky and hard to navigate.

points.name: Social Media
points.desc: Easy access for browsing, but information is unstructured and not always reliable for search.

points.name: Traditional Media
points.desc: Trusted, but slow and limited interactivity for information retrieval.

solution.name: BharatKhoj (Proposed Solution)
solution.desc: Hyper-localized, voice-first, visually intuitive search for India's diverse users.

personas.name: Rohan Sharma
personas.quote: I need quick, precise answers, often in English, but sometimes local context is key.
personas.bio: Rohan is a busy software engineer who relies on the internet for work, learning, and leisure. He's comfortable with technology but gets frustrated by irrelevant results or slow loading times. He often searches for technical documentation, local events, and news, sometimes switching between English and Hindi.
frustrations: Too much noise in search results

personas.name: Priya Singh
personas.quote: I just want to find recipes or local shop details easily, preferably by speaking.
personas.bio: Priya is a homemaker who uses the internet primarily for daily needs like recipes, local services, and educational content for her children. She's less comfortable typing in English and prefers voice search in Hindi or local dialects. She values simplicity and clear, direct answers.
needs: Reliable information for daily tasks (recipes, local shops)
frustrations: Complex interfaces with too many options
Search results that aren't directly relevant to her spoken query

insights: "Where can I find a good tailor near me?",
"How do I say 'thank you' in Tamil?",
"I wish I could just speak my question instead of typing.",
"This search bar looks simple, but will it understand my local dialect?" (referencing the visible search bar)
"Are these results relevant to *my* city, or just generic?",
"I hope I don't have to type too much, my English isn't great.",
"Is there a quicker way to get answers than scrolling through links?",
Types a query into the central search bar (visible UI element).
Clicks on the microphone icon for voice input (visible UI element).
Scrolls through search results, looking for familiar names or local addresses.
Uses 'Google offered in: Hindi Bengali Telugu...' links to switch language (visible UI element).
Frustrated when results are not locally relevant or in an unfamiliar language.
Overwhelmed by too many text links to sift through.
Relieved when voice search works accurately and provides direct answers.
Hopeful for a simpler, more direct way to get information.

points.action: Substitute text-based search as the primary input with voice-first and image-first input, making the microphone and camera icons the most prominent interaction points.

stages.actions: User needs a plumber for a leaky tap.
stages.friction: Unsure where to start looking for a reliable local service.

stages.touchpoints: Web browser (Google.com homepage visible), central search bar, microphone icon.
stages.actions: User opens browser, sees the Google logo and search bar. Tries typing 'plumber near me' or clicks microphone icon to say 'mujhe plumber chahiye' (I need a plumber).
stages.friction: Typing in English might be difficult; voice recognition might struggle with accent/dialect, leading to misinterpretation.

stages.touchpoints: Search results page (implied), snippets, map results.
stages.actions: User scans results, looking for phone numbers, addresses, or reviews. Might see generic results first, not specific to their locality.
stages.friction: Results might be too broad, not specific to their city/neighborhood, or in a language they don't fully understand. Too many links to sift through.

stages.touchpoints: Search bar again, local directory websites, asking friends on WhatsApp.
stages.actions: User might add city name to query, or give up and ask a friend. Might try clicking on 'Images' or 'Google apps' if they think it helps (visible UI elements).
stages.friction: Time-consuming, requires multiple attempts, feeling of inefficiency and lack of direct answers.

stages.actions: User eventually finds a reliable plumber's contact, makes a call, and schedules a visit.
stages.friction: The journey to get here was arduous and required significant effort due to initial search limitations.
